title: African Elephant
text: Weathered — Docked on the side of the Chobe, this lone bull slowly walked past our boat. I went on the bow and leaned as far as I could to get the head on shot. He came about 20 meters from me and just kept moving down the river indifferent to our presence. This portrait was a shot that I had envisioned for months and was so happy when I had the chance to capture it.

title: Projects
text: Investigating the potential of a X-linked genetic disorder causing the death of young male deer on Martha’s Vineyard.

title: Projects
section: Leptospira Diversity and Abundance in Fishers
text:
Leptospira Diversity and Abundance in Fishers
Investigating the number of Fishers infected with leptospira as well as the diversity of the leptospira.

title: Projects
section: Spatial and Diet Impacts on the Microbiota of New England Wild Canids
text:
Spatial and Diet Impacts on the Microbiota of New England Wild Canids
Determining if diet composition alters the microbiome of wild caninds or if spatial differences cause a greater difference.

title: Unessay
text:
I am Zeb Antonioli. I am originally from Montana and am currently studying Microbiology at the University of New Hampshire. I originally got the photography bug in 2018 on a trip to New Zealand. From there, I was hooked. I have done multiple genres of photography; however, my favorites are astrophotography and wildlife photography. This unessay assignment is meant to marry my academic interests with my passion of wildlife photography. Over the course of the past 6 years, I have had the privilege of photographing many different animals, and here I will present relevant papers and research that has been conducted specifically on the microbiome or portion of the microbiome of various species I have photographed. I will accompany this information with images I have taken along with a personal artist statement. I hope to return to my wildlife photography work as it has been put on the back burner due to my academics. Ideally, I will be able to pursue both once I graduate. Please enjoy the supplementary images below of my astrophotography work!
Timeless
Playa Selfie
Flying By

title: Unessay
text: My current goals are to obtain my PhD in Microbiology with a primary focus on wildlife conservation, ecology, and disease. My primary focuses are on genetic disease, impacts on the normal microbiome, and emerging diseases within various wildlife species. I hope to work with African wildlife once I have obtained my PhD. This unessay assignment is meant to marry my academic interests with my passion of wildlife photography. Over the course of the past 6 years, I have had the privilege of photography many different animals and here I will present relevant papers and research that has been conducted specifically on the microbiome or portion of the microbiome of various animal species. I will accompany this information with images I have taken along with a personal artist statement.

title: About Me
text:
Undergraduate
I received my B.A. from Carroll College Helena MT in 2022. I double majored in Biochemistry and Molecular Biology (BMB) as well as Biology.
Graduate
I am currently a Microbiology PhD Graduate Student at the University of New Hampshire. I am within the College of Life Sciences (COLSA) and Agriculture and the department of Molecular, Cellular, and Biomedical Sciences (MCBS). I am in my second year of the program. I am also a Graduate Student Teaching Assistant for General Microbiology.

title: About Me
text:
Undergraduate Research
I worked on monitoring for West Nile Virus presence in mosquito pools across the state of MT. This included RNA extractions and qPCR. I also performed a comparative gene expression experiment to determine if the expression of a pigmentation gene differed across populations of mosquitoes in MT.
Molecular Diagnostics
I worked as a molecular diagnostic laboratory technician for the MTVDL. I worked with various infectious agents of high agricultural impact such as High Pathogenic Avian Influenza. This involved high throughput extractions and qPCR as well as customer service with clients and delivering results in a timely manner.
Graduate Research
I have many projects occuring as of now which can be found under my Projects tab.

title: African Elephant
section: Introduction
text:
Introduction
Loxodonta africana is arguably one of the most charismatic megafauna species alive. Elephants have always captivated me since I was young. From the sheer size to the complex social structures, they remind me that we are not the only intelligent species on this earth. In 2023 I went to both Botswana and Kenya. In Chobe National Park near Kasane Botswana, the elephant populations are more tolerant of humans when compared to those of South Africa and eastern Africa. The lack of hunting and human wildlife conflict has allowed for these individuals to be more relaxed around people. This is not to say they are tame as I found out when one of the other boats was charged and tusked by a rogue male. I was informed this one of only a handful of instances of elephants behaving aggressively in this region. My experiences thankfully were not of the terrifying kind. One moment I was unable to photograph was our boat drifting close to an island in the channel of the river. On this island there was a small herd feeding with the matriarch and her calf near the water. I cannot say for certain, but we came within only mere meters of the matriarch. Nothing can describe watching as she flared her ears out and her calf hid behind her. I still get giddy thinking about that moment. Kenya was slightly less magical; however, I cannot understate how incredible being able to create artwork of these animals is.

Beach Solitude — Docked on the side of the Chobe, this lone bull slowly walked past our boat. I went on the bow and leaned as far as I could to get the head on shot. He came about 20 meters from me and just kept moving down the river indifferent to our presence. ## A metagenomic survey of the fecal microbiome of the African savanna elephant (Loxodonta africana). Preez et al., 2024
The Preez et al., paper investigated the fecal microbiome of semi-captive elephants from South Africa ranging in both age and sex. The DNA from each individual elephant’s feces was extracted and 16S rRNA gene and ITS2 were used for PCR to amplify the bacterial/archaeal and fungal components of the fecal microbiome respectively. Sequencing and subsequent analysis of these amplicons allowed the researchers to establish the relative abundance of each major taxa within the fecal microbiome. Subsequent metagenomic shotgun sequencing was performed to determine what functional genes were present that could be attributed to nutrient metabolism and acquisition. From the amplicon analysis, microbial community profiles were obtained providing a baseline characterization of savanna elephant microbiome components. This in of itself is a novel analysis and characterization for the bacterial, archaeal, and fungal communities. The metagenomic analysis showed that the majority of the catabolic pathway genes were associated with carbohydrate degradation. This result is novel yet substantiated by previous research of other hindgut fermenters such as horses and rhinos. Overall, the research provided baseline fecal microbiome profiles as well as an in depth exploration of the various carbohydrate catabolic genes present.

Flooded Lunch — While exploring the Chobe, there were many instances where we came across spread family groups that had crossed the river to access food as can be seen by the high water mark on the mother’s side. This cow calf pair were busy munching away at the waterlogged grass. I enjoy being able to sit and enjoy simple moments like foraging.

Just Like Mom — There is a special place in my heart each time we encountered young calves within a group. While the older individuals paid us no mind, the calves always stuck close to mom. It creates wonderful intimate moments. The overexposed vegitation really allows the details both on the cow and the calf to stand out making the image pop.

title: African Elephants
section: A metagenomic survey of the fecal microbiome of the African savanna elephant (Loxodonta africana). Preez et al., 2024
text:
A metagenomic survey of the fecal microbiome of the African savanna elephant (Loxodonta africana). Preez et al., 2024
The Preez et al., paper investigated the fecal microbiome of semi-captive elephants from South Africa ranging in both age and sex. The DNA from each individual elephant’s feces was extracted and 16S rRNA gene and ITS2 were used for PCR to amplify the bacterial/archaeal and fungal components of the fecal microbiome respectively. Sequencing and subsequent analysis of these amplicons allowed the researchers to establish the relative abundance of each major taxa within the fecal microbiome. Subsequent metagenomic shotgun sequencing was performed to determine what functional genes were present that could be attributed to nutrient metabolism and acquisition. From the amplicon analysis, microbial community profiles were obtained providing a baseline characterization of savanna elephant microbiome components. This in of itself is a novel analysis and characterization for the bacterial, archaeal, and fungal communities. The metagenomic analysis showed that the majority of the catabolic pathway genes were associated with carbohydrate degradation. This result is novel yet substantiated by previous research of other hindgut fermenters such as horses and rhinos. Overall, the research provided baseline fecal microbiome profiles as well as an in depth exploration of the various carbohydrate catabolic genes present.

title: African Elephant
section: Effects of diet, habitat, and phylogeny on the fecal microbiome of wild African savanna (Loxodonta africana) and forest elephants (L. cyclotis). Budd et al., 2020
text:
Effects of diet, habitat, and phylogeny on the fecal microbiome of wild African savanna (Loxodonta africana) and forest elephants (L. cyclotis). Budd et al., 2020
In this study, savanna elephants were sampled from Kenya while forest elephants were sampled from Gabon. Fecal samples were collected and DNA was extracted. Microsatellites were used to determine how many distinct individuals were sampled. Subsequent individuals’ fecal extracts were used for 16S rRNA gene metabarcoding to determine the bacterial composition of the fecal community. It was found that the dominant taxa between the two species differed substantially most likely due to the differing diets and environments being occupied. This was also reflected when members of the same species were compared with beta diversity metrics as it was found that microbiome compositions differed most when factored for habitat and diet. These findings are substantiated by the Muegge et al., paper that showed diet is one of the primary drivers in microbiome composition (2011).

Baby of Mine — This was an image I did not realize I had shot. Many of the images I show I know when I take the photo it is going to be a good image. This image I found while editing a few months later and when I finished editing it, I cried. Very few of my images move me in that way, but this image with the framing of the baby between the two cows and the trunk reaching up under their mother’s jaw, it just moved me. This image has become one of my all time favorites.

Motion — Focusing on part of the whole offers a different perspective. The mud being propelled off of the foot and the swing of the trunk are not a typical image of an elephant yet unmistakably is part of the whole.

title: African Elephant
section: References
text:

title: zebra
text: Desperation — These were a few of the brave zebra that decided to swim the Mara river. I slowed the shutter speed to incorporate more motion blur as a way to smooth the water and to provide the direction of motion. With the sharp Zebra that I was panning on as I was shooting and the other two blurred, I think it works well overall.

title: zebra
section: Introduction
text:
Introduction
There are three main species of zebra but the only species I have encountered so far is Equus quagga. This species is also known as the plains zebra and spans across large swaths of East and South Africa (King & Moehlman, 2016). Being a herd animal, zebras are often more challenging to photograph. Attempting to marry the chaos within a herd or the brief moments one is separated as a single subject makes for long waits to get the perfect shot. This is also true when waiting for them to cross a river. While in the Maasai Mara, we waited for hours to see if a large herd would cross. Eventually a brave few crossed the river allowing for some unique single shots rather than the mass exodus that the Mara river is so well known for. I got to play with some slower shutter speeds creating motion blur as they raced to cross the river which given the size of the crocodiles we saw made a whole lot more sense. I am drawn to monochrome photos, yet I often like photos of zebra in color. It makes for some pops of color with beautiful black and white contrast.

Matching Stripes — These two zebra were standing around after some significant rainfall in the Kalahari. Something about them being side by side and the added motion of the tail made this image almost like a juxtaposition between the two one focused on me and the other letting his attention be focused elsewhere.

title: zebra
section: Determinants of microbiome composition: Insights from free-ranging hybrid zebras (Equus quagga × grevyi). Abraham et al., 2024.
text:
Determinants of microbiome composition: Insights from free-ranging hybrid zebras (Equus quagga × grevyi). Abraham et al., 2024.
Abraham et al., sampled feces from Equus quagga, Equus grevyi, and their hybrid offspring that occur in Kenya. Feces underwent DNA extraction and PCR for diet and 16S bacterial microbiome. The results showed that both species had distinct microbial communities and that the hybrids tended to be more similar or nested within the microbial composition of the maternal Equus quagga. These results also showed some interesting correlations to location and feeding ecology; however, the authors noted this could be an artifact of the sampling methods as the diet of the Equus quagga differed between locations yet their microbiome compositions remained extremely similar. These results shed light on better understanding if the microbiome is more determined by environment or maternal lineage. In this case, it seems to be more influenced by maternal lineage which has been documented extensively in wild mice; however, more investigation into the social or herd influence must be done as in the same wild mouse study it was shown that both maternal and social interactions played key roles in microbiome composition (Wanelik et al., 2023).

Ford the River — This was another one of the few zebra that swam the Mara river, but the water spray created even more drama within this image.

Bold Stripes — While on a game drive, we came across some very curious yet wary zebra in the Kalahari. The challenging lighting being almost high noon made this better for black and white and overexposing the image to keep the details within the zebra that were in the partial shade.

title: zebra
section: References
text:
References
Abraham, J. O., Lin, B., Miller, A. E., Henry, L. P., Demmel, M. Y., Warungu, R., Mwangi, M., Lobura, P. M., Pallares, L. F., Ayroles, J. F., Pringle, R. M., & Rubenstein, D. I. (2024). Determinants of microbiome composition: Insights from free-ranging hybrid zebras (Equus quagga × grevyi). Molecular Ecology, 33, e17370. https://doi.org/10.1111/mec.17370

Wanelik, K. M., Raulo, A., Troitsky, T., Husby, A., & Knowles, S. C. L. (2023). Maternal transmission gives way to social transmission during gut microbiota assembly in wild mice. Animal Microbiome, 5, Article 29. https://doi.org/10.1186/s42523-023-00247-7

title: baboon
text: Stoic — It was late afternoon on the Chobe and a group of baboons had come down to drink and socialize by the river. The congregated by some bluffs that were very white. I decided to use the white backdrop to create this minimalist portrait that reminds me of a charcoal drawing while keeping amazing detail.

title: baboon
section: Introduction
text:
Introduction
Papio ursinus or the chacma baboon is the only baboon species I have had the pleasure of photographing as they are the only species that occur in Botswana (Hoffmann & Hilton-Taylor, 2008). These primates are a hoot to watch in a group. From juveniles being stinkers and adults just trying to have some peace and quiet, I understand why family get togethers with the kids often end up with someone being put in time-out. A cross species commonality as a way to put it. Of my few encounters, a favorite part was watching them as they came down to the river to drink and groom one another. The babies cradled in their mothers’ arms and the big males keeping watch with all of the small social interactions that were just constant organized chaos. I hope to get some more time with groups of baboons as I only had a small number of encounters while in Botswana and none when I was in Kenya.

Silhouette — I had maybe ten seconds to capture this image. The sun had just started to go down behind the trees near the river and through the gaps this beam hit this lone baboon and created this amazing rim lighting. I saw it, shot as many shots as I could and just as quickly as it appeared, the light vanished. I was thrilled to see I had gotten this image especially given the almost pondering way the baboon is sitting.

title: baboon
section: Human-influenced diets affect the gut microbiome of wild baboons. Moy et al., 2023.
text:
Human-influenced diets affect the gut microbiome of wild baboons. Moy et al., 2023.
Moy et al., explored how access to human diet items in the form of trash affected the microbiome of olive baboons (Papio anubis) found within Akagera National Park in Rwanda. Fecal samples were taken from three baboon groups with no access to trash, limited access to trash, and unlimited access to trash. The DNA was extracted from the feces and PCR was used to amplify 16S amplicons for sequencing the bacterial fecal microbiome. It was found that overall diversity of the fecal microbiome of those with unlimited access to trash was lower than both those with limited and no access to trash. This pattern has been observed in other studies of various animal species with one notable study on black bears indicating this is a broader anthropogenic impact on multiple species and not just a few (Gillman et al., 2022).

Mom’s Touch — The low light of this image made capturing a less noisy image impossible. This was captured late in the afternoon as the sun was setting. This mother and baby were perched on a log as the rest of the troop was running around. The poor baby was so tired and just wanted to sleep as they sat by the water. The black and white rendering helps control the noise a little better and makes it more appealing.

title: baboon
section: Social networks predict gut microbiome composition in wild baboons. Tung et al., 2015.
text:
Social networks predict gut microbiome composition in wild baboons. Tung et al., 2015.
Tung et al., investigated through shotgun metagenomics how social interactions affect the fecal microbiome of yellow baboons (Papio cynocephalus) in Amboseli National Park Kenya. Feces was collected, extracted for DNA, and sequenced to allow for the both bacterial identification and understanding the functional genes present in the microbial community. It was found that social group membership and grooming social network had strong correlations with the composition of the microbiome. This not only showed that the overall dissimilarity between those in the same social group and those that groomed one another was lower, but that also the functional genes found in the fecal microbiome were enriched in those that had stronger social interactions within their group indicating that social interactions play a key role in the functional repertoire of the fecal microbiome.

title: baboon
section: References
text:
References
Gillman, S. J., McKenney, E. A., & Lafferty, D. J. R. (2022). Human-provisioned foods reduce gut microbiome diversity in American black bears (Ursus americanus). Journal of Mammalogy, 103(2), 339–346. https://doi.org/10.1093/jmammal/gyab154
Hoffmann, M., & Hilton-Taylor, C. (2008). Papio ursinus. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2008: e.T16022A5366717. https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2008.RLTS.T16022A5366717.en

Tung, J., Barreiro, L. B., Burns, M. B., Grenier, J.-C., Lynch, J., Grieneisen, L. E., Altmann, J., Alberts, S. C., Blekhman, R., & Archie, E. A. (2015). Social networks predict gut microbiome composition in wild baboons. eLife, 4, Article e05224. https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.05224

title: bison
text: Spring Reflections — The spring in Yellowstone National Park offers bison herds on the move. I knew this pond would have a large amount of snow melt so I waited for this lone bison to be close enough to create this reflection. This is an image that I am both happy yet not fully content with because I think I can capture a better one in the future, but it is currently still one of my top five favorites.

title: bison
section: Introduction
text:
Introduction
Bison bison are a symbol of the western United States. Before westward expansion, herds would take days to pass through valleys (Epp & Dyck, 2002). Now after the near extermination of them, there are some scattered herds in various National Parks, Monuments, and private conservation areas (Sanderson et al., 2008). Growing up in Montana only a few hours away from Yellowstone National Park, I became quite acquainted with these charismatic creatures. From being stuck in Bison Jams to having groups pass alarmingly close when set up on the side of the road, I have had many amazing encounters with these animals. While many tourists want to go pet the fluffy cows, I like to keep my distance, but this has not stopped me from not keeping track of my surroundings while shooting and all of a sudden here passes a cow bison 20 feet from me just plodding along. Thankfully I haven’t been yelled at by any angry park rangers yet, but there is always next time.

Red Dog — Bison calves are sometimes referred to as red dogs. Why? I am not sure, however this baby was tired from running around the prairie and had to take a little nap by this boulder. I like this image, but it is another example of where re-composing or waiting for another shot may have yielded a result I am happier with. I will say I do like the environmental nature of the image and it incorporating more of the overall landscape around the subject.

title: bison
section: Subspecific variation in gut microbiota of North American bison in a sympatric setting reveals differentially abundant taxa. Grant et al., 2025.
text:
Subspecific variation in gut microbiota of North American bison in a sympatric setting reveals differentially abundant taxa. Grant et al., 2025.
Grant et al., investigated the gut microbiota of the two North American subspecies of Bison bison, Bison bison bison (plains bison) and Bison bison athabascae (wood bison). Fecal samples were collected from herds of both subspecies in Elk Island National Park Alberta, Canada. The feces was extracted and using short tandem repeat genotyping individual bison were identified to avoid replicates of the same individual. Subsequent metabarcoding via PCR amplification and sequencing of 16S amplicons was performed to determine the fecal microbiome composition. There were some interesting findings including significantly different alpha diversity metrics when comparing sex and species of the bison indicating some potential species and sexual differences in the overall richness and evenness of the fecal microbiome; however, the overall community similarities were still very close especially when factoring in sex. When comparing the two subspecies via Bray-Curtis Dissimilarity, it was found that they differed significantly; however, the authors noted that this could have been due to the subspecies dispersion in the area rather than true biological differences. Overall, it was found that while there were differences observed between the two subspecies, these differences are potentially due to sample collection bias and not due to biological differences.

Mom’s Shadow — Just like cattle calves, bison calves stick close to their mom not only for sustenance, but also for protection. This baby was glued to their mom and mirrored her movements as they gradually went across the prairie. I hope to go back during the spring to try and get some closer images either framing the calf between the mother’s legs or suckling.

title: bison
section: Seasonal Shifts in Diet and Gut Microbiota of the American Bison (Bison bison). Bergmann et al., 2015.
text:
Seasonal Shifts in Diet and Gut Microbiota of the American Bison (Bison bison). Bergmann et al., 2015.
Bergmann et al., investigated how seasons shifts in feeding behavior affected the bacterial microbiome of plains bison. Feces was collected from April until September from the bison herd found on the Konza Prairie Biological Station in Kansas. PCR was performed to amplify both diet content and bacterial microbiome via trnL and 16S primers respectively. These amplicons were sequenced and analyzed to determine what shifts occurred over the time span in both the diet and microbiome. What was found is that diet shifts drastically over the course of the season while the microbiome remains fairly static with small changes in specific taxa that are potentially due to diet changes, but the exact relationship remains uncertain. This study incorporates the importance of understanding the temporal changes in the gut microbiome as it is a living community that is subject to change over time especially as diet items change over the course of the seasons.

title: bison
section: References
text:
References
Bergmann, G. T., Craine, J. M., Robeson, M. S., 2nd, & Fierer, N. (2015). Seasonal Shifts in Diet and Gut Microbiota of the American Bison (Bison bison). PloS one, 10(11), e0142409. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0142409
Epp, H. T., & Dyck, I. G. (2002). Early human-bison population interdependence in the Plains ecosystem. Great Plains Research, 12(2), 323–337.7

title: African lion
text: Golden Hour — While in the Maasai Mara in Kenya, I had an amazing morning surrounded by a pride of lions. I saw this lioness moving through the grass and the contrast produced by the back lighting was incredible.

title: African lion
section: Introduction
text:
Introduction
Panthera leo are the largest terrestrial predators in Africa (Bauer et al., 2015). Coined the “King of the Jungle”, most know them to be ferocious powerful predators to be feared however, my experience both in Botswana and Kenya made me realize that lions often behave like over-sized house cats that could still kill you if they wanted to. From sunbathing, big stretches, and lots of naps, they were a joy to observe and photograph. There may also be something off with me as one particular evening in the Kalahari of Botswana I got to be up close and personal with a large male. I was alone in my vehicle with the guide and other cars started to pull up to get a look. This particular male was one of two brothers that were the leaders of one of the prides in the region. In the distance you could hear his brother roaring, and he replied. There is something incredible about feeling a lions roar reverberating through your body. While I heard other tourists start to quickly ask their guides to get them out of there, my guide Spike turned to me, saw me smiling like a fool, and kept the vehicle right there. It is a moment I will never forget.

Rest — After a long drive, we found a pride of lions living the dream. As I said before, they act like large house cats that could kill you if they wanted with this being a prime example of how my cat sleeps at home.

title: African lion
section: Individual variation within parasite communities of endangered African lions. Harris & Hallam, 2025
text:
Individual variation within parasite communities of endangered African lions. Harris & Hallam, 2025
Harris and Hallam collected carnivore fecal samples from Senegal, Benin, and Burkina Faso. The fecal samples were then used for DNA extraction and species determination via cytochrome b to move confirmed lion specific samples. The lion samples were then used for metabarcoding to determine the haemoparasite, nemabiome, and bacterial communities found in the lion feces. The most important finding was the presence of Sacocystis and Ancylostoma which can cause death in certain cases. These parasites combined with the added pressure from human encroachment and conflict are major concerns for the conservation of West African Lions. The parasite differences between populations with added human pressures is still not well understood; however, habitat fragmentation and anthropogenic pressures have been shown to decrease overall parasite diversity especially those with more complex life-cycles while increasing the carriage of others (Lafferty, 2012). Understanding this in the context of African lions is important for proper conservation efforts and monitoring.

Soulful Eyes — I encountered this shaggy male on the last night of my trip in Kenya. He and a female were on their honeymoon and offered some beautiful portrait opportunities. The shallow depth of field really helps him stand out from the grass in the background and the eyes being in perfect focus makes this image just that much better offering crisp details and a beautiful background blur.

title: African lion
section: The Gut Microbiome Profile of Lions in Etosha National Park, Namibia. Belger et al., 2026
text:
The Gut Microbiome Profile of Lions in Etosha National Park, Namibia. Belger et al., 2026
This research is currently available as a preprint. The study focused on lions within Etosha National Park in Namibia and performed shotgun metagenomics on the extracted feces. Both long and short read sequencing was done to assemble MAGs of the the bacterial species within the feces. This allowed for both an in depth functional analysis of the bacterial community and characterization of the bacterial community itself. This study is important because it focuses on wild lions rather than captive and produced both a very in depth analysis of the bacterial community structure and the functional pathways being used by the bacterial occupants. They found that not only were there multiple novel species making up variable percentages of the bacterial community, but they also found enriched purine metabolism. The characterization of these novel microbiome community members shows that additional research needs to be conducted to determine who these mystery members are.

Through the Grass — The black mane on this male was one of his notable characteristics. This was one of two brothers ruling a pride in the Kalahari of Botswana. He was on patrol duty making sure the other pride did not encroach on his and his brother’s territory. The grass offers some nice out of focus almost bokeh giving this image some more depth rather than creating a mess.
